title: Kolyvagin's conjecture and higher congruences of modular forms
speaker: Naomi Sweeting
abstract: Given an elliptic curve E, Kolyvagin used CM points on modular curves to construct a system of classes valued in the Galois cohomology of the torsion points of E. Under the conjecture that not all of these classes vanish, he gave a description for the Selmer group of E. This talk will report on recent work proving new cases of Kolyvagin's conjecture. The proof builds on work of Wei Zhang, who used congruences between modular forms to prove Kolyvagin's conjecture under some technical hypotheses. We remove many of these hypotheses by considering congruences modulo higher powers of p.

proof_stages.label: Kolyvagin classes and the finiteness conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up CM/Heegner points on modular and Shimura curves, Kolyvagin's derived cohomology classes c_m(n), the decreasing invariants m_r, and Kolyvagin's conjecture that their limiting vanishing threshold m_infinity is finite, together with its Selmer-rank consequences.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem and motivation for higher congruences
proof_stages.summary: The main result is announced as the finiteness of m_infinity under technical hypotheses, and the speaker explains via the rank-one/Tamagawa situation, Wei Zhang's work, and Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin consequences why higher congruences of modular forms should control cases with m_infinity greater than one.

proof_stages.label: Two-index Kolyvagin system and downward descent
proof_stages.summary: The original Kolyvagin system is embedded into a larger family c_m(n,q) with a level-raising index q, horizontal Kolyvagin relations and vertical level-changing relations are used to descend from a top nonvanishing class to level one, and the accumulated error is controlled by taking the congruence precision m large enough.

proof_stages.label: Level raising for mod p^m eigenforms
proof_stages.summary: Level-raising sets are defined by factorization of the mod p^m Hecke-eigenvalue map through the new quotient, the non-liftability of such maps is emphasized, and a level-raising theorem with a precise Helm-isogeny error is proved using the comparison between modular Jacobians, Shimura curves/sets, and Ihara's lemma.

proof_stages.label: Construction of generalized Kolyvagin classes from mod p^m forms
proof_stages.summary: For admissible inert auxiliary primes, CM points y(n,q) on Shimura curves are turned into cohomology classes by passing through a noncanonical map to the minimal Jacobian, taking Kummer images, applying the mod p^m level-raised Hecke map, and then using Kolyvagin derivative operators to descend to K.

proof_stages.label: Rank-zero base-case estimate
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a characteristic-zero rank-zero modular form g at the top level congruent to f, Gross's special-value formula and rank-zero BSD/Iwasawa theorems bound the localization of c_m(1,q) from below by m minus a fixed constant.

proof_stages.label: Deformation-theoretic production of the top lift
proof_stages.summary: Ramakrishna-style deformation theory is used to choose auxiliary Steinberg primes so that the relevant Selmer and dual-Selmer obstructions vanish, producing the characteristic-zero lift with prescribed local behavior needed for the rank-zero base case, while the speaker also explains the large-image hypotheses and possible modern upgrades.

examples.name: rank-one BSD/Tamagawa case and Wei Zhang's mod-p result
examples.note: The rank-one-over-K case gives a formula for m_infinity in terms of Tamagawa factors, and the comparison with Wei Zhang's m_infinity=1 result makes visible why larger m_infinity should require higher congruences rather than only mod-p congruences.
examples.engagement_reason: the rank-one BSD/Tamagawa formula and Wei Zhang’s mod-p theorem are stated as known inputs/motivation without deriving the formula or working through a case

examples.name: Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin converse in Selmer rank one
examples.note: The speaker uses the rank-one Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin theorem and its converse as an application showing the arithmetic significance of proving Kolyvagin's conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the converse step by step from Selmer rank one through Kolyvagin’s bounds to ν=0, nonvanishing of CM(1), non-torsion of y_k, and analytic rank one

examples.name: two-eigenvalue non-normal Hecke algebra toy model
examples.note: A quotient with one Hecke operator having eigenvalues p and 2p illustrates how maps to Z/p^2 can be Frankenstein mixtures that do not lift to characteristic zero, motivating the need to treat mod p^m eigenforms directly.
examples.engagement_reason: the toy Hecke algebra is explicitly analyzed by computing maps to Z/p^2, explaining the choices of eigenvalues, and comparing with the normalization

passes.reason: The non-normal two-branch Hecke algebra is a collapsed congruence toy model showing mod-p^m maps need not lift.
The non-normal two-eigenvalue Hecke algebra has collapsed congruent eigenstructure mod p and is used to show mod p^m eigenmaps need not lift to characteristic zero.
The non-normal two-branch Hecke algebra has congruent eigenvalue branches collapsed modulo p and is used to show mod p^m maps need not lift to characteristic zero.
The two-branch non-normal Hecke algebra has eigenbranches collapsed modulo p and is used to show mod p^m Hecke maps need not lift.

examples.name: ordinary versus supersingular primes in the rank-zero input
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the Kolyvagin-system argument is uniform except that the rank-zero Iwasawa/BSD input comes from different ordinary and supersingular main-conjecture theorems.

examples.name: small-prime boundary cases p=3 and p=2
examples.note: A question about excluded small primes elicits the comparison that p=3 may be manageable by absorbing congruence defects into the error term, whereas p=2 creates serious BSD, multiplicity-one, and deformation-theoretic obstacles.
examples.engagement_reason: the small-prime cases are analyzed by checking how the auxiliary-prime congruence behaves for p=3 and why p=2 breaks multiple inputs
